coreJobStatement: Diagnose, treat, and stabilize patients presenting with acute medical and surgical conditions
productJobStatement: Help Facility & Building Safety to maintain authorized access boundaries at emergency exits while guaranteeing single-motion escape compliance across all clinical zones, and provide real-time alerts on unauthorized activation events across the campus
lPath: L7 Ecosystem > L6h1 Facility & Building Safety > L5 Emergency Exit Security Device

segments.name: Psychiatric / behavioral health inpatient
segments.characteristics: Patient on involuntary hold, suicide watch, or acute behavioral episode; exit = life-safety failure
segments.targetabilityReason: RTLS wander tags vs. door alarm guard vs. wired ACS are non-interchangeable
segments.circumstance: Patient on involuntary hold, suicide watch, or acute behavioral episode; exit = life-safety failure
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — RTLS wander tags vs. door alarm guard vs. wired ACS are non-interchangeable

segments.name: Cognitively impaired inpatient
segments.characteristics: Dementia, delirium, TBI — wanders without understanding consequences
segments.targetabilityReason: Physical door barrier vs. wearable geofence serve different needs
segments.circumstance: Dementia, delirium, TBI — wanders without understanding consequences
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — physical door barrier vs. wearable geofence serve different needs

segments.name: Pediatric / NICU patient
segments.characteristics: Infant or minor requiring protection against unauthorized removal
segments.targetabilityReason: Infant RF protection systems vs. exit alarm guards are distinct categories
segments.circumstance: Infant or minor requiring protection against unauthorized removal
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — infant RF protection systems vs. exit alarm guards are distinct categories

segments.name: Controlled-access zone occupant
segments.qualifier: Secondary — primary alternatives are full ACS systems
segments.characteristics: Staff moving through OR, pharmacy, blood bank, sterile supply — identity verification required
segments.targetabilityReason: Full ACS (credential + lock) vs. exit alarm-only are distinct jobs
segments.circumstance: Staff moving through OR, pharmacy, blood bank, sterile supply — identity verification required
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — full ACS (credential + lock) vs. exit alarm-only are distinct jobs

segments.name: General inpatient
segments.characteristics: Medical-surgical ward — compliance-driven, not clinical containment
segments.targetabilityReason: Basic mechanical alarm vs. IoT-monitored exit differ on documentation value
segments.circumstance: Medical-surgical ward — compliance-driven, not clinical containment
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — basic mechanical alarm vs. IoT-monitored exit differ on documentation value

segments.name: Facility & building safety manager
segments.characteristics: Manages exit compliance, JCI audits, maintenance across multi-wing campus
segments.targetabilityReason: Manual patrol vs. centralized IoT dashboard are operationally distinct
segments.circumstance: Manages exit compliance, JCI audits, maintenance across multi-wing campus
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — manual patrol vs. centralized IoT dashboard are operationally distinct

alternatives.name: Wired alarmed exit bars (Von Duprin, Corbin Russwin, Sargent)
alternatives.tradeoffs: Requires conduit and cable at every door; licensed electrician; disruptive retrofit in occupied wards; no wireless campus dashboard

alternatives.name: Mechanical alarm guards, no IoT (GfS Basic, ABUS)
alternatives.tradeoffs: Zero remote monitoring; alarm event discoverable only on-site; no automated compliance documentation; maintenance requires physical inspection rounds

alternatives.name: Wireless electronic exit control (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic)
alternatives.tradeoffs: Higher unit cost; typically BACnet-integrated; more complex configuration and commissioning; heavier BMS dependency

alternatives.name: RTLS wander management (Securitas Healthcare, CenTrak, RF Technologies)
alternatives.tradeoffs: Patient-worn tag required; no physical door barrier; covers elopement but does not satisfy exit compliance documentation; ~3–5x higher system cost

alternatives.name: Manual patrol + paper inspection logs [Status Quo]
alternatives.tradeoffs: Zero device cost; 100% labor-dependent; no real-time detection; patrol gaps expose liability; documented compliance gaps risk JCI non-conformance findings

jobSteps.description: Specify compliance requirements, door count, zone classifications, and wireless topology per NFPA 101 / JCI / EN 179/1125 standards
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Variant selection (EN 179 vs. EN 1125, IoT vs. basic) is determined at spec stage
jobSteps.rationale: Specifying compliance requirements, door count, zone classifications, wireless topology per NFPA 101/JCI standards

jobSteps.description: Map all fire-code-designated emergency exit doors across wards, wings, and floors — often 50–500+ doors in legacy multi-wing hospitals
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Wireless topology design requires door inventory and RF coverage assessment
jobSteps.rationale: Mapping 50–500+ exit doors across wards, wings, and floors — often incomplete in legacy buildings

jobSteps.description: Install devices on door hardware and commission wireless receiver/gateway network without disrupting occupied clinical zones
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Retrofit installation without conduit is the product's key advantage; installation quality determines coverage
jobSteps.rationale: Retrofit installation in occupied clinical zones without disrupting patient care

jobSteps.description: Verify alarm function, wireless coverage, key-override operation, and establish compliance documentation baseline before go-live
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Coverage gaps and compliance baseline must be confirmed before clinical handover
jobSteps.rationale: Testing alarm function, wireless coverage gaps, documentation baseline before go-live

jobSteps.description: Operate continuously: secure exits, trigger alarms on unauthorized activation, process authorized key overrides, maintain fail-safe mechanical alarm independence
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Core operating function; elopement detection and false alarm rate are primary performance KPIs
jobSteps.rationale: Core function: securing exits, triggering alarms, processing key overrides

jobSteps.description: Centrally observe real-time door status, alarm events, and battery levels across campus from dashboard; detect anomalies before they become incidents
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: IoT monitoring is the primary value differentiator over mechanical-only alternatives
jobSteps.rationale: Centralized real-time status visibility across campus — the primary IoT value

jobSteps.description: Respond to alarm events with location-specific dispatch; reconfigure devices when ward functions change; replace batteries on maintenance schedule
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Ward reorganizations and battery management are ongoing operational burdens in hospital settings
jobSteps.rationale: Responding to alarm events, ward reconfigurations, battery replacements

jobSteps.description: Generate JCI Environment of Care documentation, validate 12-month operational logs, schedule preventive maintenance cycles, and close compliance audit cycles
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Automated compliance documentation is the core ROI justification for IoT variant over manual inspection
jobSteps.rationale: JCI survey documentation, compliance log generation, preventive maintenance scheduling

stakeholders.who: Facilities / engineering technician operating and maintaining devices
Facilities Director, Safety Officer, Chief Compliance Officer
Hospital patients (psychiatric, dementia, pediatric) — protected from harm
Security integrator (install), facilities tech (maintain), fire safety inspector (validate)
Joint Commission surveyor, risk manager, infection preventionist

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect product variant specification (EN 179 vs. EN 1125) causing non-conforming door installation identified at first JCI fire-safety survey
Increase the ability to document a complete hospital door inventory (type, standard, zone classification) across a 300-bed campus within 2 working days
Minimize variability in wireless signal strength across reinforced-concrete hospital floors that causes >5% of installed devices to fall below reliable reporting threshold
Minimize installation time per door in occupied inpatient wards to <30 minutes without requiring conduit, cables, or a licensed electrician
Minimize the likelihood of installation failure on non-standard door hardware configurations (glass-frame doors, narrow lever handles, legacy panic bars) in pre-1990 hospital wings
Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered post-installation requiring costly repeater additions to achieve full-campus alarm reporting coverage
Increase the ability to verify single-motion door release compliance per EN 179 / EN 1125 for 100 installed devices within a 5-working-day JCI preparation window
Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized emergency exit activation going undetected for >5 minutes during overnight or weekend reduced-staffing periods
Minimize the frequency of false alarms on high-traffic emergency exits exceeding 2 events per week due to incidental door contact by clinical staff

Increase the ability to provide authorized clinical staff with immediate key-bypass access on emergency exits during acute clinical emergencies in <10 seconds without triggering a facility-wide alarm response
Increase the ability to monitor battery status for all exit devices across a 200+ door hospital campus from a single dashboard with >95% real-time accuracy
Minimize the likelihood of alarm event transmission failures exceeding 1% due to RF interference from hospital imaging equipment (MRI suites, CT rooms) in adjacent zones
Increase the ability to correlate emergency exit alarm events with concurrent patient elopement incidents in the incident reporting system with <30 seconds of timestamp resolution
Minimize the time required to reconfigure exit device zone assignments when hospital wards are repurposed or patient population mix changes, to <2 hours per ward
Minimize the effort required to replace batteries across all campus exit devices within a planned maintenance window without disrupting clinical operations in adjacent rooms

Increase the ability to generate Joint Commission-compliant Environment of Care documentation for all emergency exit security devices covering 12 months of operational logs without manual data aggregation
Minimize the likelihood of GDPR-regulated door event logs containing data attributable to identifiable individuals without a legally established processing basis under EU 2016/679
Minimize variability in alarm trigger response time across door types (EN 179 lever-handle vs. EN 1125 panic-bar configurations) to within ±0.2 seconds under equivalent activation force
Increase the ability to demonstrate real-time emergency exit compliance status to hospital leadership and external surveyors on demand, without manual inspection rounds
Minimize the likelihood of visible emergency exit compliance gaps during an unannounced Joint Commission Environment of Care survey
Minimize the delay between an emergency exit activation event and receipt of an actionable location-specific alert on the security officer's device to <15 seconds

sources.label: NFPA 101 Life Safety Code — Hospital Occupancy Chapter 18
Joint Commission Environment of Care Standard EC.02.06.01
PSNet AHRQ — Patient Elopement Safety Data
